speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Based on your recent labs and clinical evaluation, I’m diagnosing you with Chronic Kidney Disease Stage 3, confirmed as of your visit on October 25th.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure was 87 mmHg during that same visit — slightly elevated, so we’ll monitor that closely.

speaker: doctor
text: Your fasting blood glucose came back at 14.0 mmol/L — significantly above target, which raises concern for uncontrolled diabetes.

speaker: patient
text: That’s higher than I expected. I’ve been trying to watch my diet, but I’ve also had more fatigue and frequent urination lately.

speaker: doctor
text: Those are classic symptoms of hyperglycemia — and given your CKD stage, managing blood sugar becomes even more critical to protect your kidneys.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been checking my glucose at home sometimes — but I’m not sure how often or when is best.

speaker: doctor
text: On the same day, your systolic blood pressure was 120 mmHg — so overall BP was 120 over 87. That diastolic number is what we’re most concerned about right now.

speaker: doctor
text: We’re starting you on Metformin 1000 mg twice daily, as it’s generally safe in early CKD and helps improve insulin sensitivity.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve heard Metformin can cause stomach upset — should I take it with food?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, absolutely — always take it with meals to minimize GI side effects. Also, avoid it if you develop vomiting or dehydration, especially since your kidney function is reduced.

speaker: patient
text: Got it. And how often should I check my blood sugar now?

speaker: doctor
text: Let’s start with fasting and two hours after your largest meal — and bring your log to our next visit in four weeks.

speaker: patient
text: I’ll do that. My sister has diabetes too — she told me her A1c was 8.2 last month. Mine was 9.4 last week.

speaker: doctor
text: That aligns with your glucose level — an A1c of 9.4% roughly correlates with an average glucose around 13–14 mmol/L. We’ll aim to get that below 7.5% given your CKD.

speaker: patient
text: Is there anything else I should be watching for — like swelling or changes in urine?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — watch for foamy urine, reduced output, new swelling in your ankles or face, or shortness of breath. Those could signal worsening kidney function or fluid overload.

speaker: patient
text: Okay. I’ve also noticed some mild leg cramps at night — could that be related?

speaker: doctor
text: Possibly — electrolyte shifts or volume depletion can contribute, especially with high glucose levels pulling fluid into the bloodstream. Let’s check your potassium and magnesium next time.

speaker: patient
text: Makes sense. I checked my glucose again this morning before breakfast — same number as before.

speaker: patient
text: It was 14.0 mmol/L — just like that reading from October 25th.
